---
title: "Runtime Adapters, Tool Permissions, and Human Approval"
chapter_id: "runtime-adapters-tool-permissions-and-human-approval"
part_id: "planning-memory-reasoning-execution"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "talos"
  - "viea"
  - "ladon_manhattan"
  - "software_magic_grimoire"
  - "genesiscode"
  - "moecot"
  - "field_of_god_ai_constitution"
  - "theseus_operator_os"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Appendix C now records exact source-note mappings for the core claim; support remains at argument until adapter execution, sandbox, approval-service, rollback, or secret-handle harnesses exist."
  - "Protocol schema/fixture validation and narrow Lean permission/approval predicates are implemented; no adapter execution, sandbox, approval-service, rollback, or secret-handle harness has been run for this chapter."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Runtime Adapters, Tool Permissions, and Human Approval

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `runtime-adapters-tool-permissions-and-human-approval` |
| Part | Part II - Planning, Memory, Reasoning, and Execution |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `talos`, `viea`, `ladon_manhattan`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`, `moecot`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `theseus_operator_os` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `talos`, `viea`; supporting: `ladon_manhattan`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `theseus_operator_os`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `talos`, `viea`, `ladon_manhattan`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`, `moecot`, `field_of_god_ai_constitution`, `theseus_operator_os`; raw cache: `talos`, `viea`, `ladon_manhattan`, `software_magic_grimoire`, `genesiscode`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | Runtime adapter fixture validation implemented; finite permission and approval predicates implemented in `AsiStackProofs.RuntimeAdapters`; adapter execution, sandbox, approval-service, rollback, and secret-handle tests remain planned. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter treats runtime adapters as effect-boundary records, not as proof that tools were safely executed. The repo validates an adapter-invocation fixture and now proves two finite Lean predicates over permission and approval records, but the fixture does not call a tool, inject a secret, enforce a live approval service, or produce a real rollback handle.

## Problem

Plans become real-world effects only through tools, runtimes, deployment adapters, and approval gates.

Typed jobs are still inert until a runtime adapter performs an effect. This chapter defines the boundary where work touches tools, APIs, filesystems, services, hardware, fabrication targets, organizational workflows, or deployment environments. The adapter is where permissions, sandboxing, approvals, secrets, result evidence, and rollback handles must be enforced.

Talos supplies the controlled runtime and approval discipline. Ladon supplies handle-mediated authority and secret isolation. GenesisCode supplies capability boundaries and effect logs. Theseus Operator OS adds the operator-surface requirement: mutating actions should be visible, bounded, and revocable.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Tool calling without a permission and approval model turns reasoning errors into side effects.

A tool call is not just cognition. It can delete a file, spend money, send a message, deploy code, move a robot, leak a secret, or change an institutional state. If approval and permission are implicit, every planning error can become a real-world action.

The right boundary is not "never call tools." It is "call tools through adapters whose capabilities, approval requirements, authority handles, result evidence, and rollback options are explicit."

## Core Claim

[runtime-adapters-tool-permissions-and-human-approval.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Runtime adapters should enforce typed permissions, sandboxing, human approval, and post-action evidence capture.

The claim remains at `argument` support. Talos supplies adapter and approval discipline; VIEA supplies intent-to-runtime and feedback boundaries; Ladon/Manhattan supplies handle-mediated secret use; GenesisCode supplies capability-mediated effects and replay logs; Software Magic Grimoire and Theseus Operator OS supply workflow and operator-surface pressure. MoECOT remains a connector implementation-reference route, not local execution evidence.

### Claim-source mapping status

Appendix C now maps this runtime-adapter claim to all assigned source notes. The mappings support typed permissions, sandbox and approval boundaries, handle-mediated authority, least-power governance, operator visibility, and post-action evidence capture, not safe tool execution in this repository.

| Source | What it supports | Limit |
|---|---|---|
| `talos` | Typed jobs, tool isolation, contract locks, human approval, blind handles, Digital SCIFs, evidence records, audit logs, replay, tests, residuals, and regressions. | No live adapter runner, approval service, sandbox, security test, or Talos runtime result has been reproduced. |
| `viea` | Structured command contracts, runtime adapters, verified outputs, deployment feedback, residuals, tools, benchmarks, and regression coverage. | No deployed runtime adapter, feedback loop, or verified VIEA execution trace exists here. |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Hardened secret managers, opaque credential handles, policy-mediated injection, ephemeral compartments, zeroization, outbound-call interception, and side-channel assumptions. | Security architecture only; no Ladon implementation, kernel-level test, side-channel validation, or security audit exists here. |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Guarded entry/exit rules, handoff artifacts, verification and failure behavior, incompatible-mode separation, evidence-advancing loops, and scoped recursion. | Workflow vocabulary only; does not prove adapter enforcement, approval quality, or tool safety. |
| `genesiscode` | Capability-mediated effects for I/O, time, randomness, networking, model calls, deterministic effect logs, and protocol seals. | No local capability runtime, sandbox, effect log verifier, or security proof exists. |
| `moecot` | Specialist lanes, fail-closed control-plane ledgers, readiness gates, replay, handoff, promotion blockers, and authority-sensitive runtime orchestration. | Runtime artifacts and benchmark/replay logs remain uninspected and unreproduced. |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Governed tool use, power, memory, delegation, consent, reversibility, least sufficient power, risk tiers, auditability, human review, and no self-authorization. | Constitutional specification only; no runtime policy engine, red-team suite, system-prompt evaluation, or proof was run here. |
| `theseus_operator_os` | Shared command vocabulary, durable work board, node registry, background/watch/wake contracts, skill registry, tool hooks, feedback routing, TTLs, kill switches, signed updates, and isolation. | No Hive board, SQLite database, node registry, command channel, hook ledger, or dashboard was run from this repository. |

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Runtime Adapter Invocation.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Typed job"] --> B["Adapter contract"]
  B --> C["Capability check"]
  C --> D["Authority / secret handle"]
  D --> E{"Approval required?"}
  E -- "yes" --> F["Human approval record"]
  E -- "no" --> G["Sandboxed invocation"]
  F --> G
  G --> H["Effect receipt"]
  H --> I["Rollback handle / residual"]
  I --> J["Artifact graph"]
```

- Map jobs to runtime adapters with declared capabilities.
- Cover document/software, API/service, firmware/hardware, CAD/fabrication, robotics, and organizational workflow targets where source support exists.
- Require permission checks and approval gates for high-impact actions.
- Record deployment evidence and rollback handles.

Adapters should declare target type, capability, permission required, sandbox mode, approval requirement, input/output contract, authority handles, expected evidence, rollback handle, and post-action audit events. The tool itself is not the policy boundary; the adapter is.

High-impact actions include irreversible deployments, external messages, monetary transfers, credential use, safety-relevant hardware, and institutional actions. These should fail closed without approval.

## Interfaces

The interface is the Runtime Adapter Invocation.

- Execution owns adapters.
- Security mediates secrets.
- Evidence records deployment outcomes.

Minimum fields:

- `invocation_id`
- `job_id`
- `adapter_id`
- `target_type`
- `capability`
- `permission_required`
- `sandbox_mode`
- `approval_required`
- `approval_record`
- `authority_handle`
- `inputs`
- `effect_receipt`
- `rollback_handle`
- `residuals`

Execution owns adapters. Security mediates secrets and authority handles. Evidence records effect receipts, rollback handles, residuals, and deployment outcomes.

## Invariants

- No adapter executes beyond granted scope.
- High-impact actions require approval.
- Rollback handles are captured when available.

No adapter invocation is valid unless the parent job permissions include the adapter capability. A high-impact adapter call without approval is rejected before the tool receives authority.

## Failure modes

- Tool overreach.
- Approval bypass.
- Irreversible deployment without rollback.

Tool overreach means the adapter performed beyond granted scope. Approval bypass means governance was skipped. Irreversible deployment without rollback means a failed action cannot be repaired. These failures should produce effect residuals and block future route promotion.

## Minimal implementation

A runtime-adapter contract with permissions, approval requirements, and result evidence.

The smallest useful implementation is a `runtime_adapter_invocation` schema and fixture. It validates adapter ID, capability, permission, sandbox, approval, authority handle, effect receipt, rollback handle, and residual fields. It does not execute a tool.

The fixture validates the invocation envelope. `AsiStackProofs.RuntimeAdapters` adds a bounded permission predicate showing that a valid invocation requires the parent job permission list to include the adapter capability, plus a bounded approval predicate requiring a high-impact unapproved invocation to be rejected. This does not demonstrate tool execution, sandbox isolation, human approval workflow, secret-handle safety, effect receipt validity, or rollback.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Runtime adapter fixture validation | Check that the adapter fixture matches the public schema and declares job, adapter, target, capability, permission, sandbox, approval, authority handle, inputs, effect receipt, rollback handle, and residuals. | implemented by protocol validation; run in this pass |
| Adapter permission predicate | Check that adapter capability is included in the parent job's permissions. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.RuntimeAdapters`; checked by Lean build |
| Approval gate rejection predicate | Check that high-impact adapter calls without approval are rejected. | implemented in `AsiStackProofs.RuntimeAdapters`; checked by Lean build |
| Rollback handle capture test | Check that deployment/effect records capture rollback handles or residuals. | planned; not run |

Fixture-shape validation and two bounded Lean predicates are implemented. The remaining tests require an adapter harness, sandbox, parent job permission lookup against real jobs, high-impact classification, logged approval decisions, and effect receipts.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:runtime.adapters.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.RuntimeAdapters` | A runtime adapter invocation is valid only when job permissions include the adapter capability. | implemented |
| `lean:runtime.adapters.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.RuntimeAdapters` | A high-impact adapter call without approval is rejected. | implemented |

These proof hooks are implemented as finite-record predicates. They do not prove sandboxing, authority-handle mediation, actual tool invocation, effect receipt validity, or rollback behavior.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `viea` | Verified Intent-to-Execution Architecture | whole_stack_execution_spine | Keystone source. Human intent -> command contracts -> artifacts -> routing -> runtime targets -> verification -> deployment -> feedback. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `ladon_manhattan` | Ladon & The Manhattan Protocol | security_governance | Kernel-level security architecture for high-agency AI. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `software_magic_grimoire` | Software Magic Grimoire | command_contracts_promptcraft | Structured command contracts and semantic density of software words. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `genesiscode` | GenesisCode | executable_specification | Tiny pure calculus + obligations + provenance for auditable AI-symbiotic programming. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |
| `field_of_god_ai_constitution` | Field of God AI Constitution | constitutional_alignment_runtime_governance | Recovered in the Project Theseus repository. Constitutional alignment core for truth alignment, agency preservation, consent, non-domination, consciousness caution, least sufficient power, auditability, self-authorization limits, and runtime checks; use as source material only after source-note creation, not as proof or test evidence. | source note available |
| `theseus_operator_os` | Hive Operator OS and Work Board | labor_os_operator_surface | Shared command vocabulary, durable SQLite work board, node registry, background/watch/wake contracts, skill registry, tool hooks, feedback routing, and safety-visible operator surface. | source note available |

The crosswalk identifies sources for effects, authority handles, approval gates, and operator-visible control surfaces. It does not claim safe tool execution has been demonstrated here.

## Summary

Runtime Adapters, Tool Permissions, and Human Approval is the effects boundary. It maps jobs to adapters, checks permissions, mediates authority handles, enforces approval gates, invokes tools in bounded modes, records effect receipts, and captures rollback or residuals.

The chapter's rule is that real-world effects require explicit authority. Planning may propose action; only an authorized adapter may perform it.
